body:
## Review Summary

Clean approach — parameterizing the assembly via `global_asm!` template operands eliminates 855 lines of duplicated assembly and is more maintainable than the previous two-file solution. The macro, curly-brace escaping, and `sym` operand usage are all correct.

Main concern: the `elf` detection flag is too narrow now that the outer `#[cfg]` gate has been broadened (see inline at mod.rs line 2).

comments.body:
The outer `#[cfg]` was broadened from `all(target_arch = "aarch64", target_os = "linux"/"macos")` to just `target_arch = "aarch64"`, which means this now compiles on any aarch64 platform (FreeBSD, Android, Windows, etc.). But `cfg!(target_os="linux")` only matches Linux, so on e.g. FreeBSD or Android (both ELF platforms) the `elf` flag would be `0`, triggering the Mach-O address resolution path — which will fail to assemble.

Two options:
1. Keep the broad gate but fix the flag: `elf = const cfg!(not(target_vendor = "apple")) as u32` (Mach-O is the exception, ELF is the default)
2. Restore the narrow gate: `#[cfg(all(target_arch = "aarch64", any(target_os = "linux", target_os = "macos")))]`

Option 1 is more forward-compatible.

nit: shadowing the ARM built-in `adr` instruction with this macro is intentional and works here since all uses in the file need the long-range `adrp+add` pair. Worth a brief comment that this macro intentionally overrides the built-in `adr` pseudo-instruction — future readers (or contributors adding new code to this file) might be surprised that `adr` doesn't emit the actual `adr` instruction.
